channel.title: The Weekly Challenge - RSS Feed
channel.description: Get regular The Weekly Challenge RSS Feed.

item.title: RECAP - The Weekly Challenge - 378 | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Thank you Team PWC for your continuous support and encouragement.

item.title: The Weekly Challenge - 379 | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Welcome to the Week #379 of The Weekly Challenge.

item.title: The Weekly Challenge - Guest Contributions | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: As you know, The Weekly Challenge, primarily focus on Perl and Raku. During the Week #018, we received solutions to The Weekly Challenge - 018 by Orestis Zekai in Python. It was pleasant surprise to receive solutions in something other than Perl and Raku. Ever since regular team members also started contributing in other languages like Ada, APL, Awk, BASIC, Bash, Bc, Befunge-93, Bourne Shell, BQN, Brainfuck, C3, C, CESIL, Chef, COBOL, Coconut, C Shell, C++, Clojure, Crystal, CUDA, D, Dart, Dc, Elixir, Elm, Emacs Lisp, Erlang, Excel VBA, F#, Factor, Fennel, Fish, Forth, Fortran, Gembase, Gleam, GNAT, Go, GP, Groovy, Haskell, Haxe, HTML, Hy, Idris, IO, J, Janet, Java, JavaScript, Julia, K, Kap, Korn Shell, Kotlin, Lisp, Logo, Lua, M4, Maxima, Miranda, Modula 3, MMIX, Mumps, Myrddin, Nelua, Nim, Nix, Node.js, Nuweb, Oberon, Octave, OCaml, Odin, Ook, Pascal, PHP, PicoLisp, Ploki, PostgreSQL, Postscript, PowerShell, Prolog, Python, R, Racket, Rexx, Ring, Roc, Ruby, Rust, Scala, Scheme, Sed, Smalltalk, SQL, Standard ML, SVG, Swift, Tcl, TypeScript, Typst, Uiua, V, Visual BASIC, WebAssembly, Wolfram, XSLT, YaBasic and Zig.

item.title: PostgreSQL MVCC - Part 3 | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Having done two parts, Part 1 and Part 2, of the series on PostgreSQL MVCC, here is the final post of the series.

item.title: RECAP - The Weekly Challenge - 373 | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Thank you Team PWC for your continuous support and encouragement.

item.title: PostgreSQL MVCC - Part 2 | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: In the previous post, I discussed the Transaction Isolation Levels in PostgreSQL. In this post, we will explore how it is managed under the hood.

item.title: The Weekly Challenge - 378 | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Welcome to the Week #378 of The Weekly Challenge.

item.title: RECAP - The Weekly Challenge - 377 | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Thank you Team PWC for your continuous support and encouragement.

item.title: PostgreSQL and MVCC | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: I started my programming career with Oracle as my first database, before moving to MySQL, which I used for a long time. Later I returned to Oracle for another five years. Eventually, I transitioned to PostgreSQL, and it has been my database of choice ever since. Overall, I have worked with PostgreSQL more than any other database system.

item.title: RECAP - The Weekly Challenge - 376 | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Thank you Team PWC for your continuous support and encouragement.

item.title: Webhook using Perl | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Sometimes ago, I created a blog post on the subject, WebSocket. The post titled, Mojolicious with WebSocket, was well received. So I decided to create a dedicated GitHub repository. It allowed me to re-create the application using different backends as listed below:

item.title: Roles in core Perl OO | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: After years of architectural debates and meticulous implementation, the first Minimum Viable Product (MVP) phase of Corinna was officially merged into the core runtime with the release of Perl 5.38.0 under the use feature 'class' guard.

item.title: Mutation in GraphQL | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: In the last post, CPAN and GraphQL, I shared the new CPAN module DBIx::Class::Schema::GraphQL. It was the third post in the series so far. In all the post so far, I have only used CREATE and READ operation. Although the newly created CPAN module does support mutation but I didn’t mention that in the previous post.

item.title: The Weekly Challenge - 377 | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Welcome to the Week #377 of The Weekly Challenge.

item.title: CPAN and GraphQL | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: On the subject of GraphQL, I have written two blog posts so far:

item.title: DBIx::Class and GraphQL | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Two days ago, I wrote a blog post titled, Mojo meets GraphQL. It was my first time using GraphQL and it was mostly well received.

item.title: Mojo meets GraphQL | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: I was first introduced to GraphQL when I found out that a friend of mine, Pete Sergeant, of Perl Careers sponsored the work GraphQL in 2017.

item.title: RECAP - The Weekly Challenge - 375 | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Thank you Team PWC for your continuous support and encouragement.

item.title: JSON-RPC with Perl | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Just a few days ago, Dave Cross, published an article titled Teaching AI About the British Monarchy with MCP. It reminded me to finally get started on MCP, something I have been delaying for so long. While reading through the article, I noticed JSON-RPC being discussed quite prominently.

item.title: CPAN Debian Package | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: I remember when I used to work with ActiveState Perl, I always struggled to find a PPM for CPAN modules. I reached the point where I decided to create the missing PPMs myself, but I never managed to create one, not even once.

item.title: The Weekly Challenge - 376 | Jun 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Welcome to the Week #376 of The Weekly Challenge.

item.title: RAG with Perl | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: The term RAG has bothered me for a long time. I have heard many people around me talking about it. I am not an AI enthusiast, but last June I wrote a blog post about AWS Bedrock. A friend of mine at work was working with it at that time and it piqued my interest.

item.title: Result Class - Components | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: For my upcoming book on DBIx::Class, I have been working on examples to support the thoeretical chapters. This exercise helped me fill several gaps in my narration. I started with DBIx::Class - Result Class & ResultSet Class followed by a three-part series on Schema Class:Â Part 1, Â Part 2 Â andÂ Part 3.

item.title: RECAP - The Weekly Challenge - 374 | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Thank you Team PWC for your continuous support and encouragement.

item.title: Strategy in Python | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: My formal introduction to Python was about two years ago when I took some courses by LinkedIn Learnings. Since I don’t use Python on a daily basis, I tend to forget things. Luckliy, participating in The Weekly Challenge helps me keep my skills sharp.

item.title: The Weekly Challenge - 375 | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Welcome to the Week #375 of The Weekly Challenge.

item.title: RECAP - The Weekly Challenge - 295 | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Thank you Team PWC for your continuous support and encouragement.

item.title: REST vs gRPC | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: There was a time when I was working on a legacy, very large monolithic system. Then there was a change in policy, we decided to create microservice heavily using Docker. That was the first time, I was formally introduced to term microservice. This was about 10 years ago, time just flies.

item.title: RECAP - The Weekly Challenge - 302 | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Thank you Team PWC for your continuous support and encouragement.

item.title: Strategy Design Pattern | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: In this blog post, I am talking about Strategy design pattern in Perl. The example used in the post is taken from my book, Design Patterns in Modern Perl.

item.title: RECAP - The Weekly Challenge - 304 | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Thank you Team PWC for your continuous support and encouragement.

item.title: The Weekly Challenge - 374 | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Welcome to the Week #374 of The Weekly Challenge.

item.title: OAuth2 with Dancer2 | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Last night, I published a blog post about JWT with Dancer2. It was something that I never had chance to play with. I thoroughly enjoyed the journey.

item.title: JWT with Dancer2 | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: I remember the good old days of web application when we used sessions to remember user because the underlying protocol of the web is completely stateless. This was the fallback mechanism when cookie support is disabled or unavailable on the client’s browser.

item.title: Big O Notation | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Before I start, let me give you some background: I hold an Honours Degree in Mathematics. During my degree program in 1993, I completed a one-year Diploma in Information and System Management course. That was the only formal technical education I ever received, and even then I was only taught COBOL and FoxPro. Everything else I have learned has been through books or, later, by looking up materials online.

item.title: The Weekly Challenge - 373 | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Welcome to the Week #373 of The Weekly Challenge.

item.title: Explain act | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Just to give you context, Gabor Szabo, while working on opensource projects recently, he picked up my latest creation DBIx::Class::Async. He noticed, I didn’t have workflow configuration file. He kindly created pull request and submitted: PR #3. Without wasting a moment, I accepted the pull request and merged.

item.title: DBIx::Class - Schema Class (Part 3) | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: This is the third part of the series post about Schema class. I would highly recommend you checkout the earlier posts first: Part 1 and Part 2.

item.description: Thank you Team PWC for your continuous support and encouragement.

item.title: The Weekly Challenge - 372 | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Welcome to the Week #372 of The Weekly Challenge.

item.title: DBIx::Class - Schema Class (Part 2) | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: In my previous post, I discussed about Schema class ability to assign default_resultset_class. In this post I am focussing on two more features i.e. connection() and exception_class().

item.title: RECAP - The Weekly Challenge - 318 | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Thank you Team PWC for your continuous support and encouragement.

item.title: DBIx::Class::Async - Oracle Database | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: It has been a long time since I shared an update regarding DBIx::Class::Async. The reason is simple: no known critical issues have been reported. While DBIx::Class::Async v0.65 is officially still experimental, it is quite stable. I am not in a rush to declare it production ready just yet.

item.title: RECAP - The Weekly Challenge - 320 | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Thank you Team PWC for your continuous support and encouragement.

item.title: RECAP - The Weekly Challenge - 319 | May 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: Thank you Team PWC for your continuous support and encouragement.

item.title: DBIx::Class - Schema Class | Apr 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: In my earlier post, I discussed about Result and ResultSet class. In this post I am focussing on the Schema class. I know, this feels a bit backward!

item.description: Thank you Team PWC for your continuous support and encouragement.

item.title: Testing in Perl | Apr 2026 | The Weekly Challenge
item.description: If you’re a fan of Test Driven Development (TDD) like I am, you have likely explored many different ways to test your code. I’m not ashamed to call myself an old school student; for a long time, I relied on my good old friend, Test::More, for most of my testing.
